---
layout: post
title: "Finite, Infinite, Countable and Uncountable Sets"
date: 2026-03-25
category: maths
lang: en
lang_pair: "/blog/countable-and-uncountable-sets-hindi/"
permalink: /blog/countable-and-uncountable-sets-english/
description: "Finite vs infinite; countably infinite ℕ, ℤ, ℚ; uncountable ℝ; Cantor's diagonal argument; Schroeder–Bernstein theorem — with 4 worked examples for CSIR NET, GATE, IIT JAM."
---

<style>
  .math-post{font-family:Georgia,serif;font-size:1.05rem;line-height:1.9;color:#2d2d2d;max-width:860px;margin:0 auto}
  .post-hero{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1e3c72 0%,#2a5298 50%,#7c3aed 100%);border-radius:16px;padding:38px 35px;color:white;margin-bottom:35px;position:relative;overflow:hidden}

  .app-icon{font-size:1.6rem}

  .pdf-btn{display:inline-flex;align-items:center;gap:14px;background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1B2A4A,#2A5298);color:white;padding:15px 36px;border-radius:6px;font-family:Georgia,serif;font-size:1rem;font-weight:700;box-shadow:0 4px 18px rgba(27,42,74,.40);transition:transform .2s,box-shadow .2s;text-decoration:none}
  .pdf-btn:hover{transform:translateY(-3px);box-shadow:0 8px 26px rgba(27,42,74,.50);color:white;text-decoration:none}
  .pdf-btn .pi{font-size:1.5rem}
  .pdf-btn .pt{display:flex;flex-direction:column;text-align:left;line-height:1.35}
  .pdf-btn .pt1{font-size:1rem}
  .pdf-btn .pt2{font-size:.72rem;opacity:.82;font-weight:400}
  @media(max-width:700px){.card-hierarchy{grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr}.revision-grid{grid-template-columns:1fr}}
</style>

<div class="math-post">

<!-- §1 HERO + STATS -->
<div class="post-hero">
  <h1>Finite, Infinite, Countable and Uncountable Sets</h1>
  <p>How do you compare the "sizes" of infinite sets? The integers seem larger than the naturals, the rationals denser than both — yet all three are the same size. The reals, however, are genuinely larger. This post builds the complete theory of cardinality: what it means for two sets to have the same size, which infinite sets are countable, and why $\mathbb{R}$ is fundamentally different from $\mathbb{Q}$.</p>
  <a class="lang-switch" href="/blog/countable-and-uncountable-sets-hindi/">&#x1F1EE;&#x1F1F3; हिंदी में पढ़ें</a>
</div>

<div class="stats-strip">
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">ℵ₀</span><span class="stat-lbl">Countable infinity</span></div>
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">𝔠</span><span class="stat-lbl">Continuum</span></div>
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">ℵ₀ &lt; 𝔠</span><span class="stat-lbl">Key inequality</span></div>
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">4</span><span class="stat-lbl">Solved Examples</span></div>
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">CSIR/GATE/JAM</span><span class="stat-lbl">Exam Relevance</span></div>
</div>

<!-- §2 DEFINITIONS -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#ede9fe;">&#x221E;</div>
  <h2>Finite, Infinite, Countable, Uncountable — Definitions</h2>
</div>

<div class="info-card purple">
  <div class="info-card-header purple">&#x1F4D0; Core Definitions</div>
  <p>Two sets $A$ and $B$ are <strong>equinumerous</strong> ($A \sim B$) if there exists a bijection $f \colon A \to B$. Equinumerosity is the precise way to compare sizes of sets — for infinite sets it replaces naive counting.</p>
  <p><strong>Finite:</strong> $A$ is finite if $A = \emptyset$ or $A \sim \{1,2,\ldots,n\}$ for some $n \in \mathbb{N}$. Write $|A| = n$.</p>
  <p><strong>Infinite:</strong> $A$ is infinite if it is not finite. Equivalently (Dedekind): $A$ is infinite iff it is equinumerous with a proper subset of itself.</p>
  <p><strong>Countably infinite (denumerable):</strong> $A \sim \mathbb{N}$. Write $|A| = \aleph_0$.</p>
  <p><strong>Countable:</strong> $A$ is finite or countably infinite.</p>
  <p><strong>Uncountable:</strong> $A$ is infinite but NOT $\sim \mathbb{N}$.</p>
</div>

<div class="card-hierarchy">
  <div class="hier-card" style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#059669,#34d399);">
    <h4>Finite</h4>
    <p>$\emptyset$, $\{1,2,3\}$<br>$|A|=n$</p>
  </div>
  <div class="hier-card" style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#2a5298,#60a5fa);">
    <h4>Countably ∞</h4>
    <p>$\mathbb{N},\mathbb{Z},\mathbb{Q}$<br>$|A|=\aleph_0$</p>
  </div>
  <div class="hier-card" style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#7c3aed,#a78bfa);">
    <h4>Uncountable</h4>
    <p>$\mathbb{R},(0,1)$<br>$|A|=\mathfrak{c}$</p>
  </div>
  <div class="hier-card" style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#dc2626,#f87171);">
    <h4>Even larger</h4>
    <p>$\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})$<br>$|A|=2^{\mathfrak{c}}$</p>
  </div>
</div>

> 📖 <strong>Reference:</strong> Rudin, W., <strong>Principles of Mathematical Analysis</strong>, 3rd ed., Ch. 2, §§2.1–2.14. Also: Apostol, T.M., <strong>Mathematical Analysis</strong>, 2nd ed., Ch. 1, §§1.14–1.16.

<div class="info-card gold">
  <div class="info-card-header gold">&#x1F517; Prerequisites</div>
  <p><strong>&#x2190;</strong> <a href="/blog/sets-and-basic-notation-english/"><strong>Sets and Basic Notation</strong></a> — set language, power sets, Cartesian products.<br>
  <strong>&#x2190;</strong> <a href="/blog/functions-and-relations-english/"><strong>Functions and Relations</strong></a> — bijections are the core tool for comparing cardinalities.<br>
  <strong>&#x2190;</strong> <a href="/blog/logic-and-proof-methods-english/"><strong>Logic and Proof Methods</strong></a> — proof by contradiction drives Cantor's diagonal argument.</p>
</div>

<!-- §3 INTUITION -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#d1fae5;">&#x1F4A1;</div>
  <h2>Intuition — Why ℕ, ℤ, ℚ Are the Same Size, but ℝ Is Bigger</h2>
</div>

<p>The key insight is that <strong>size of infinite sets is measured by bijections, not by inclusion or density</strong>. Even though $\mathbb{N} \subsetneq \mathbb{Z} \subsetneq \mathbb{Q}$, we can match every integer to a unique natural number and vice versa — so they are the same size. The rationals, despite being dense on the number line, can also be listed one by one.</p>

<div class="info-card green">
  <div class="info-card-header green">&#x1F50D; Why ℝ is Different — Cantor's Core Idea</div>
  <p>Suppose you claim to have listed every real number in $(0,1)$: $x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots$ Cantor's diagonal trick builds a real number $y$ that <strong>differs from $x_1$ in its 1st decimal digit, from $x_2$ in its 2nd, from $x_3$ in its 3rd</strong>, and so on. This $y$ is in $(0,1)$ but is not anywhere on your list — contradicting the claim that the list was complete. No matter how cleverly you list reals, there are always more.</p>
</div>

<div class="info-card purple">
  <div class="info-card-header purple">&#x1F4D0; Key Theorems</div>
  <p><strong>Countability results (Rudin, Thm. 2.12–2.13):</strong></p>
  <ul style="margin:8px 0;padding-left:20px;line-height:1.9">
    <li>Every infinite subset of a countable set is countable.</li>
    <li>$\mathbb{Z}$ is countably infinite (explicit bijection to $\mathbb{N}$).</li>
    <li>$\mathbb{Q}$ is countably infinite (Cantor's first diagonal enumeration).</li>
    <li>A <strong>countable union of countable sets is countable</strong>: if each $A_n$ is countable, then $\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n$ is countable.</li>
  </ul>
  <p><strong>Uncountability (Rudin, Thm. 2.14):</strong> $(0,1)$ is uncountable — hence $\mathbb{R}$ is uncountable with $|\mathbb{R}| = \mathfrak{c} > \aleph_0$.</p>
  <p><strong>Schroeder–Bernstein Theorem:</strong> If injections $f \colon A \to B$ and $g \colon B \to A$ both exist, then a bijection $h \colon A \to B$ exists. So $|A| = |B|$.</p>
  <p><strong>Cantor's Power Set Theorem:</strong> For any set $A$, $|A| < |\mathcal{P}(A)|$. In particular $|\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})| = \mathfrak{c}$.</p>
</div>

<div class="info-card hindi">
  <div class="info-card-header gold">&#x1F1EE;&#x1F1F3; हिंदी में संक्षेप</div>
  <p>दो समुच्चयों का <strong>गणनांक (Cardinality)</strong> Bijection से मापा जाता है। <strong>गणनीय (Countable)</strong> समुच्चय वे हैं जिनका $\mathbb{N}$ से Bijection हो: $\mathbb{N}, \mathbb{Z}, \mathbb{Q}$ सभी $\aleph_0$ हैं। <strong>अगणनीय (Uncountable)</strong> समुच्चय: $\mathbb{R}$ की Cardinality $\mathfrak{c} > \aleph_0$ है। Cantor का विकर्ण तर्क (Diagonal Argument) Contradiction द्वारा सिद्ध करता है कि $(0,1)$ अगणनीय है। $\mathbb{R}\setminus\mathbb{Q}$ (अपरिमेय संख्याएँ) भी अगणनीय हैं।</p>
</div>

<!-- §4 SOLVED EXAMPLES -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#fef3c7;">&#x270F;&#xFE0F;</div>
  <h2>Solved Examples</h2>
</div>

<div class="example-card ex1">
  <div class="example-header">
    <div class="example-num">1</div>
    <div>
      <div class="example-label">Very Easy &nbsp;|&nbsp; Explicit Bijection</div>
      <div class="example-title">Exhibit a bijection $f \colon \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Z}$; conclude $|\mathbb{Z}| = \aleph_0$</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="solution-block">
    <p><strong>Define:</strong> $f(n) = \begin{cases} 0 & n = 1 \\ n/2 & n \text{ even} \\ -(n-1)/2 & n \text{ odd}, n \geq 3 \end{cases}$</p>
    <p><strong>Listing:</strong> $f(1),f(2),f(3),f(4),f(5),f(6),\ldots = 0, 1, -1, 2, -2, 3, \ldots$</p>
    <p><strong>Injective:</strong> Even and odd cases produce distinct non-negative / non-positive outputs; within each case $f$ is strictly monotone. $\checkmark$</p>
    <p><strong>Surjective:</strong> Every $k \geq 0$ is $f(2k)$ (or $f(1)$ for $k=0$); every $k < 0$ is $f(1-2k)$. $\checkmark$</p>
    <p>Hence $f$ is a bijection and $|\mathbb{Z}| = |\mathbb{N}| = \aleph_0$. $\blacksquare$</p>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="example-card ex2">
  <div class="example-header">
    <div class="example-num">2</div>
    <div>
      <div class="example-label">Easy–Medium &nbsp;|&nbsp; Proper Subset Same Size</div>
      <div class="example-title">Show $E = \{2, 4, 6, \ldots\}$ (positive even integers) satisfies $|E| = \aleph_0$</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <p>Note $E \subsetneq \mathbb{N}$, yet we claim they are equinumerous.</p>
  <div class="solution-block">
    <p><strong>Define:</strong> $f \colon \mathbb{N} \to E$ by $f(n) = 2n$.</p>
    <p><strong>Injective:</strong> $f(m)=f(n) \Rightarrow 2m=2n \Rightarrow m=n$. $\checkmark$</p>
    <p><strong>Surjective:</strong> Every even integer $2k \in E$ equals $f(k)$. $\checkmark$</p>
    <p>So $|E| = \aleph_0$. This is the hallmark of infinite sets: a proper subset can have the same cardinality as the whole — this is in fact Dedekind's definition of an infinite set. $\blacksquare$</p>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="example-card ex3">
  <div class="example-header">
    <div class="example-num">3</div>
    <div>
      <div class="example-label">Medium–Hard &nbsp;|&nbsp; Bijection Between Bounded and Unbounded</div>
      <div class="example-title">Construct an explicit bijection $(0,1) \to \mathbb{R}$; deduce $|(0,1)| = |\mathbb{R}| = \mathfrak{c}$</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="solution-block">
    <p><strong>Define:</strong> $f \colon (0,1) \to \mathbb{R}$ by $f(x) = \tan\!\left(\pi\!\left(x - \tfrac{1}{2}\right)\right)$.</p>
    <p><strong>Well-defined:</strong> For $x \in (0,1)$, the argument $\pi(x-\tfrac{1}{2}) \in (-\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{\pi}{2})$, where $\tan$ is defined. $\checkmark$</p>
    <p><strong>Injective:</strong> $\tan$ is strictly increasing on $(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2})$, so $f(x_1) = f(x_2) \Rightarrow x_1 = x_2$. $\checkmark$</p>
    <p><strong>Surjective:</strong> As $x \to 0^+$, $f(x) \to -\infty$; as $x \to 1^-$, $f(x) \to +\infty$. By the Intermediate Value Theorem, every $y \in \mathbb{R}$ is attained. $\checkmark$</p>
    <p>Hence $|(0,1)| = |\mathbb{R}| = \mathfrak{c}$, showing that a bounded interval and the entire real line have the same cardinality. $\blacksquare$</p>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="example-card ex4">
  <div class="example-header">
    <div class="example-num">4</div>
    <div>
      <div class="example-label">Hard &nbsp;|&nbsp; CSIR NET / GATE / IIT JAM Level</div>
      <div class="example-title">Prove $\mathbb{Q}$ is countably infinite; deduce $\mathbb{R} \setminus \mathbb{Q}$ is uncountable</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="solution-block">
    <p><strong>Part 1 — $\mathbb{Q}$ is countable.</strong></p>
    <p>Arrange all positive rationals in an infinite array: entry $(p, q)$ is $p/q$ for $p, q \in \mathbb{N}$. Traverse the array by diagonals:</p>
    <p style="text-align:center;font-family:'Courier New',monospace;font-size:.9rem;letter-spacing:.05em">
    $\frac{1}{1} \to \frac{1}{2}$ &nbsp; $\frac{2}{1} \to \frac{2}{2}$ &nbsp; $\frac{3}{1} \cdots$<br>
    $\searrow$ &nbsp;&nbsp; $\nearrow\searrow$ &nbsp;&nbsp; $\nearrow$<br>
    $\frac{1}{2}$ &nbsp;&nbsp; $\frac{2}{2}$ &nbsp;&nbsp; $\frac{3}{2} \cdots$</p>
    <p>Reading along diagonals: $\tfrac{1}{1}, \tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{2}{1}, \tfrac{1}{3}, \tfrac{2}{2}, \tfrac{3}{1}, \ldots$ — delete duplicates, prepend $0$ and negatives. This gives a surjection $\mathbb{N} \twoheadrightarrow \mathbb{Q}$, hence a bijection (since $\mathbb{Q}$ is infinite). So $|\mathbb{Q}| = \aleph_0$. $\checkmark$</p>
    <p><strong>Part 2 — $\mathbb{R} \setminus \mathbb{Q}$ is uncountable.</strong></p>
    <p>Suppose for contradiction that $\mathbb{R} \setminus \mathbb{Q}$ is countable. Then $\mathbb{R} = \mathbb{Q} \cup (\mathbb{R} \setminus \mathbb{Q})$ would be a union of two countable sets, hence countable. But $\mathbb{R}$ is uncountable — contradiction. Therefore $\mathbb{R} \setminus \mathbb{Q}$ is uncountable. $\blacksquare$</p>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §5 QUICK REVISION -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#dbeafe;">&#x26A1;</div>
  <h2>Quick Revision Cards</h2>
</div>

<div class="revision-grid">
  <div class="rev-card a">
    <h4>&#x1F4CA; A — Cardinalities &amp; Key Facts</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>$|\mathbb{N}|=|\mathbb{Z}|=|\mathbb{Q}|=\aleph_0$</li>
      <li>$|\mathbb{R}|=|(0,1)|=\mathfrak{c}>\aleph_0$</li>
      <li>$|\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})|=\mathfrak{c}$</li>
      <li>Countable ∪ countable = countable</li>
      <li>$\mathbb{R}\setminus\mathbb{Q}$ uncountable</li>
      <li>$|A|&lt;|\mathcal{P}(A)|$ for all $A$</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
  <div class="rev-card b">
    <h4>&#x2699;&#xFE0F; B — Conditions &amp; Edge Cases</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>Subset of countable = countable</li>
      <li>$\mathbb{Q}$ dense in $\mathbb{R}$ but still countable</li>
      <li>$(0,1) \subsetneq \mathbb{R}$ yet $|(0,1)|=|\mathbb{R}|$</li>
      <li>Dedekind: infinite ↔ $\sim$ proper subset</li>
      <li>Schroeder–Bernstein: two injections → bijection</li>
      <li>Diagonal argument needs decimal expansions in $(0,1)$</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
  <div class="rev-card c">
    <h4>&#x1F3AF; C — Exam Tips</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>&#x1F535; <strong>CSIR NET:</strong> To show countable: bijection to $\mathbb{N}$, or subset/union of countables</li>
      <li>&#x1F7E2; <strong>GATE:</strong> To show uncountable: diagonal argument or contains a copy of $(0,1)$</li>
      <li>&#x1F7E0; <strong>IIT JAM:</strong> $\mathbb{R}\setminus\mathbb{Q}$ uncountable — standard proof via contradiction</li>
      <li>&#x1F534; <strong>B.Sc. Raj.:</strong> State bijection and verify injective + surjective explicitly</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §6 COMMON MISTAKES -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#fee2e2;">&#x26A0;&#xFE0F;</div>
  <h2>Common Mistakes</h2>
</div>

<div class="warning-box">
  <h4>&#x274C; Errors to Avoid</h4>
  <div class="warning-item">
    <div class="warning-label">Error 1: Concluding |ℕ| &lt; |ℤ| because ℕ ⊊ ℤ</div>
    <p><strong>Wrong:</strong> "ℤ has more elements so $|\mathbb{Z}| > |\mathbb{N}|$." &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Correct:</strong> The bijection $\mathbb{N} \leftrightarrow \mathbb{Z}$ shows $|\mathbb{N}| = |\mathbb{Z}| = \aleph_0$. For infinite sets, containment does NOT imply strict cardinality.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="warning-item">
    <div class="warning-label">Error 2: Thinking density implies uncountability</div>
    <p><strong>Wrong:</strong> "$\mathbb{Q}$ is dense in $\mathbb{R}$, so it must be uncountable." &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Correct:</strong> $\mathbb{Q}$ is countable. Density (between any two reals lies a rational) is a topological property, completely independent of cardinality.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="warning-item">
    <div class="warning-label">Error 3: Claiming $(0,1)$ is smaller than ℝ because it's a proper subset</div>
    <p><strong>Wrong:</strong> "$(0,1) \subsetneq \mathbb{R}$ so $|(0,1)| < |\mathbb{R}|$." &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Correct:</strong> The bijection $x \mapsto \tan(\pi(x-\frac{1}{2}))$ shows $|(0,1)| = |\mathbb{R}| = \mathfrak{c}$.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="warning-item">
    <div class="warning-label">Error 4: Misapplying the diagonal argument to ℕ</div>
    <p><strong>Wrong:</strong> "Using Cantor's diagonal to prove $\mathbb{N}$ is uncountable." &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Correct:</strong> The diagonal argument constructs a new real from decimal expansions — it produces a number in $(0,1)$, not a natural number. It cannot be applied to $\mathbb{N}$.</p>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §7 APPLICATIONS -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#ecfdf5;">&#x1F310;</div>
  <h2>Real-World Applications</h2>
</div>

<div class="app-grid">
  <div class="app-card" style="border-top-color:#2a5298;">
    <div class="app-icon">&#x1F4BB;</div>
    <h4>Computability Theory</h4>
    <p>Programs are finite strings — countably many. Real-valued functions are uncountably many. Hence most functions are <em>not computable</em>. This is Turing's foundational insight.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="app-card" style="border-top-color:#059669;">
    <div class="app-icon">&#x1F4E1;</div>
    <h4>Information Theory</h4>
    <p>A countably infinite alphabet can be encoded in binary (each symbol gets a finite codeword). Uncountable sets cannot be encoded with finite binary strings — the basis for data compression limits.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="app-card" style="border-top-color:#7c3aed;">
    <div class="app-icon">&#x1F4D0;</div>
    <h4>Measure Theory</h4>
    <p>Countable sets have Lebesgue measure zero. $\mathbb{Q}$ has measure zero inside $\mathbb{R}$, even though $\mathbb{Q}$ is dense — the foundation of Lebesgue integration and modern probability.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="app-card" style="border-top-color:#d97706;">
    <div class="app-icon">&#x1F3B2;</div>
    <h4>Cryptography — Randomness</h4>
    <p>True randomness requires sampling from an uncountable set (physical processes modelling $\mathbb{R}$). Pseudo-random generators produce sequences from a countable set and are thus inherently periodic.</p>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §8 SUMMARY TABLE -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#e0e7ff;">&#x1F4CB;</div>
  <h2>Summary Table &amp; Key Result</h2>
</div>

<table class="summary-table">
  <thead>
    <tr><th>Set</th><th>Cardinality</th><th>How established</th><th>Reference</th></tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr><td><strong>$\mathbb{N}$</strong></td><td>$\aleph_0$</td><td>Definition</td><td>Rudin §2.4</td></tr>
    <tr><td><strong>$\mathbb{Z}$</strong></td><td>$\aleph_0$</td><td>Explicit bijection $\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{Z}$</td><td>Rudin §2.12</td></tr>
    <tr><td><strong>$\mathbb{Q}$</strong></td><td>$\aleph_0$</td><td>Diagonal enumeration</td><td>Rudin §2.13</td></tr>
    <tr><td><strong>$(0,1)$, $\mathbb{R}$</strong></td><td>$\mathfrak{c}$</td><td>Cantor's diagonal argument</td><td>Rudin §2.14</td></tr>
    <tr><td><strong>$\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})$</strong></td><td>$\mathfrak{c}$</td><td>Binary representations</td><td>Cantor's thm</td></tr>
    <tr><td><strong>$\mathbb{R}\setminus\mathbb{Q}$</strong></td><td>$\mathfrak{c}$</td><td>Contradiction via countable union</td><td>Example 4</td></tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<div class="highlight-result">
  <p><strong>The Cardinality Hierarchy:</strong></p>
  <p>$$0 \;<\; 1 \;<\; 2 \;<\; \cdots \;<\; \aleph_0 \;<\; \mathfrak{c} \;<\; 2^{\mathfrak{c}} \;<\; \cdots$$</p>
  <p>$\mathbb{N} \sim \mathbb{Z} \sim \mathbb{Q}$ (all $\aleph_0$) $\qquad\qquad$ $\mathbb{R} \sim (0,1) \sim \mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})$ (all $\mathfrak{c}$)</p>
  <p>Countable union of countable sets is countable.</p>
</div>

<!-- §9 CROSS-REFERENCES -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#fef3c7;">&#x1F517;</div>
  <h2>Cross-References &amp; Related Posts</h2>
</div>

<div class="info-card gold">
  <div class="info-card-header gold">&#x1F4DA; Prerequisites &amp; Next Steps</div>
  <p><strong>&#x2190; Prerequisites:</strong>
    <a href="/blog/sets-and-basic-notation-english/"><strong>Sets and Basic Notation</strong></a> — set language and power sets &nbsp;|&nbsp;
    <a href="/blog/functions-and-relations-english/"><strong>Functions and Relations</strong></a> — bijections &nbsp;|&nbsp;
    <a href="/blog/logic-and-proof-methods-english/"><strong>Logic and Proof Methods</strong></a> — proof by contradiction.</p>
  <p><strong>&#x2192; Next Topic:</strong> <em>Bounded Sets, Supremum and Infimum</em> — the completeness axiom of $\mathbb{R}$, which distinguishes $\mathbb{R}$ from $\mathbb{Q}$ analytically (just as uncountability distinguishes them set-theoretically).</p>
  <p><strong>&#x1F4D6; Further Reading:</strong> Rudin, Ch. 2, §§2.1–2.14; Apostol, Ch. 1, §§1.14–1.16; Bartle &amp; Sherbert, Ch. 1, §1.3.</p>
</div>
